domains.name: Language and Literacy
domains.summary: Language and literacy focuses on how children communicate, understand language, and engage with spoken and written texts. It includes growth in speech, conversation, sound awareness, comprehension, and text purpose.

behaviours.name: Single Words & Gestures
behaviours.description:
• Speaks (communicates) using a very limited range of single words.
  • May gesture instead of, or as well as, speaking.

behaviours.name: Single Words & Phrases
behaviours.description:
• Speaks (communicates) briefly using single words and 2-3 word phrases. Ideas are rarely linked.
  • Uses a limited vocabulary that may mainly be nouns.

behaviours.name: Short Linked Phrases
behaviours.description:
• Speaks (communicates) briefly in short sentences or phrases (e.g., up to 4 words) attempting to link some ideas, using a small range of simple, common words.
  • Speech (communication) may be disordered (e.g., incorrect sentence structure (syntax)) with some confusion about word meaning.
  • May be hard to follow at times.

behaviours.name: Simple Sentences
behaviours.description:
• Speaks (communicates) in sentences of up to 5-6 words. May use one or two conjunctions (mainly ‘and’) and one or two pronouns making some errors.
  • Mainly uses simple, common nouns and a limited range of verbs appropriately to convey meaning.

behaviours.name: Several Connected Sentences
behaviours.description:
• Speaks (communicates) for several sentences (e.g., 35+ words) mainly using 2-3 simple conjunctions (e.g., 'and', 'then', ‘so’)
  • Speech (communication) mostly uses correct word order (syntax).
  • Uses many common nouns, and some simple verbs and adjectives appropriately to convey meaning.

behaviours.name: Longer Sentences
behaviours.description:
• Uses some longer sentences (e.g., 7+ words) when speaking (communicating) and 3-4 common conjunctions (e.g., and, but, because, then).
  • Uses personal pronouns (e.g., ‘I’, ‘me’, ‘you’ ‘we’, ‘us’, ‘he’, she’) and a wide variety of common nouns, verbs and adjectives appropriately to convey meaning.

behaviours.name: Rich Connected Speech
behaviours.description:
• Uses many long sentences and 5-6 conjunctions (e.g., and, but, because, then, so, after) and a range of pronouns when speaking.
  • Uses a wide range of common words, including words and constructions from story books and in everyday speech.

behaviours.name: Limited Question Responses
behaviours.description: • Insufficient language to understand or respond to most simple questions or make requests.

behaviours.name: Gesture-Based Responses
behaviours.description: • Mainly relies on gestures in responding to questions or making requests (e.g., shakes head, or points).

behaviours.name: Short Personal Stories
behaviours.description:
• Tells a short personal story (child’s own experiences, thoughts or feelings) that jumps between unlinked ideas and/or events.
  • Makes a few short, simple, familiar requests (e.g., ‘More please).
  • Mostly responds to questions with a single word (e.g., yes/no).

behaviours.name: Simple Conversations
behaviours.description:
• Tells a personal story (child’s own experiences, thoughts or feelings) with details that are not distinguished from main events.
  • Has short conversations about predictable and familiar topics (e.g., what they are playing with).
  • Asks broad or general questions (e.g., ’what’, ‘where’, or ‘who’).
  • Responds briefly to a variety of questions and makes simple requests.

behaviours.name: Logical Personal Stories
behaviours.description:
• Tells a personal story (child’s own experiences, thoughts or feelings) that has a logical flow (beginning, middle and end).
  • Has conversations about a range of familiar topics mostly from their immediate context (e.g., what they are doing at the time).
  • Contributes relevant ideas.
  • Asks a range of specific questions that relate to the topic.

behaviours.name: Detailed Conversations
behaviours.description:
• Tells a longer personal story (child’s own experiences, thoughts or feelings) that is well structured, providing some details to improve or clarify the story in response to questions.
  • Has thoughtful conversations, including some less familiar topics outside their immediate context.

behaviours.name: Structured Detailed Stories
behaviours.description:
• Tells a longer, detailed personal story (child’s own experiences, thoughts or feelings) that is well structured, identifying the problem/main event and resolution, without being asked prompting questions.
  • Contributes considered ideas to conversations.
  • Asks questions that build on or clarify their understanding.

behaviours.name: Supported Word Attempts
behaviours.description: • Requires modelling and support to say recognisable words.

behaviours.name: Partial Syllable Repetition
behaviours.description:
• Repeats words of 2-4 syllables but only says some of the sounds (e.g.,child might say "elephant" as "el-fant,").
  • Everyday words are often unclear.

behaviours.name: Mostly Clear Speech
behaviours.description: • Talk is mostly clear in conversations.

behaviours.name: Clear Syllable Repetition
behaviours.description: • Repeats 2 and 3-syllable words and says all of the sounds.

behaviours.name: Clear Four-Syllable Words
behaviours.description:
• Repeats an unfamiliar 4-syllable word and says all the sounds.
  • Talks clearly in conversations.

behaviours.name: Sound, Rhyme & Syllables
behaviours.description:
• Identifies when the first sounds in words are the same and when they are not.
  • Identifies if words rhyme or not
  • Generates a word that rhymes including both real (e.g., 'cat' and 'hat') and nonsense words (e.g., 'lat').
  • Can identify syllables in words, such as by clapping.

behaviours.name: Picture-Based Story Ideas
behaviours.description:
• Uses pictures in a book to answer questions about the main characters or problem in the story.
  • Labels 3-4 very familiar objects in the illustrations.
  • Refers to one key idea in a short and simple story (e.g., the main event, the main character).

behaviours.name: Retells Simple Key Ideas
behaviours.description:
• Retells the key ideas from a short and simple text (2-3).
  • Names most of the objects in the illustrations that are key to the story.
  • Interprets simple ideas (e.g., how a character is feeling) from explicit clues or an illustration.
  • Links simple ideas (e.g., cause and effect – watering the plant makes it grow) for a predictable, familiar event.

behaviours.name: Retells and Links Ideas
behaviours.description:
• Retells the story including most key ideas from throughout a simple text.
  • Interprets simple, implied ideas (e.g., a secondary character’s feelings).
  • Links cause and effect for most key events or characters’ motivations (e.g., characters’ decision to share their toys made their friend happy).

behaviours.name: Explains Implied Ideas
behaviours.description:
• Explains a range of implied ideas (e.g., the reason for a character’s behaviour).
  • Makes simple, obvious comparisons (e.g., about a predictable change in a story – ‘The caterpillar spins a cocoon and changes into a butterfly’), and obvious predictions ('The character will find the treasure in the cave because he has a treasure map).
  • Makes links between well separated ideas (e.g., where there are several events or cumulative causes that lead to an effect – the steps to planting a seed).
  • Gives a simple reason for their opinion.

behaviours.name: Sophisticated Text Reasoning
behaviours.description:
• Explains a range of implied ideas with some sophistication (e.g., a character’s subtly communicated motivation).
  • Makes comparisons. between viewpoints (e.g., compares one character’s view to another) and makes generalisations from explicit clues.
  • Gives synonyms or antonyms for highly familiar words (e.g., mad means angry).
  • Makes links between a series of events (e.g., to sequence a short story).
  • Gives evidence to support their opinion (e.g., why a character behaved as they did).

behaviours.name: Explores Sensory Books
behaviours.description:
• Selects books with textural features such as lift-the-flaps, tactile surfaces, sounds and minimal, simple labels.
  • Attends to the sensory features of books designed for this developmental stage.

behaviours.name: Recognises Favourite Texts
behaviours.description:
• Selects a highly familiar, preferred book, holds it the right way up based on key features such as the front cover or illustrations that interest them.
  • Recognises some familiar environmental print (e.g., name of centre, logo) based on clues such as graphics and location.

behaviours.name: Identifies Familiar Text Purposes
behaviours.description:
• Identifies the purpose of highly familiar texts (e.g., this text tells a story, this one is for cooking, this is a shopping list).
  • Distinguishes factual texts from fictional texts.

behaviours.name: Identifies Text Types
behaviours.description: • Identifies the purpose of a few different text types and features (e.g., fairytales, nursery rhymes, show and tell).

behaviours.name: Identifies Many Text Purposes
behaviours.description: • Identifies the purpose of a range of familiar text types and features (e.g., Dreamtime stories, recipes, craft instructions, fables, myths, lists, labels).

domains.name: Executive Function
domains.summary: Executive function focuses on how children manage attention, persistence, adaptability, and problem solving. It supports children to plan, respond flexibly, manage challenges, and build independence in learning.

behaviours.name: Supported Task Engagement
behaviours.description:
• Observes tasks being performed by others, preparing to undertake one element of the task.
  • Completes a single element of a highly structured task (e.g., finds their bag when they need to put their bag on their back).
  • Demonstrates joint attention (e.g., in a group situation, directs their attention toa specified object when prompted).
  • Requires assistance from others to stay engaged, (e.g., provision of simple, manageable tasks).
  • Relies on routines, visual schedules and scaled prompts to initiate engagement with activities and tasks.
  • Rarely recognises a need for help.

behaviours.name: Routine Task Persistence
behaviours.description:
• Completes simple, routine tasks with one step instructions (e.g., putting their bag in their locker).
  • Maintains focus despite minor distractions (e.g., staying on task, ignoring peers playing nearby).
  • Persists through a small setback or challenge (e.g., when equipment is missing).
  • Waits for their turn in a game with one other person or when asked to line-up for a short period.
  • Requires reassurance with tasks that are new or challenging.
  • Engages best when verbal and visual prompts are provided throughouta task.
  • Requires instructions to be repeated.
  • May pause when they need help but may not initiate requests for help.

behaviours.name: Multi-Step Persistence
behaviours.description:
• Persists with tasks involving multiple steps that require some time, effort, and patience (e.g., building a block tower).
  • Maintains focused attention when engaged in preferred tasks.
  • Persists through one or two set-backs but may require support if challenges continue (e.g., trying to put shoes on)
  • Waits for their turn in a game involving two or three people (e.g., they are last in a line of children waiting and they do not express frustration verbally or physically).
  • May initiate new or challenging tasks.
  • Aware of needing help but may not always ask for it.

behaviours.name: Independent Persistence
behaviours.description:
• Persists with tasks involving more than three steps which require time, effort and patience (e.g., solving a multipiece puzzle, playing a game with several players).
  • Resists distractions when focused on a task involving several steps (e.g., background noise, peers playing close-by, and lunch is being served).
  • Attempts to manage set-backs and persist independently. May ask a peer or adult for help.
  • Waits patiently for their turn when a game or task demands it (e.g., in a game of UNO if it is their turn next, but then order is reversed, so they now have to wait longer to have their turn).
  • Seeks specific reassurance and responds appropriately to feedback when persisting with new or challenging tasks.

behaviours.name: Supports Others' Persistence
behaviours.description:
• Manages multiple different setbacks and distractions throughout complex tasks (tailored to the child’s ability).
  • Supports others by providing strategies for managing setbacks.
  • Actively congratulates others, recognising their persistence, and helps or encourages them.
  • Demonstrates patience and consideration when waiting for others to take their turn.
  • Completes multi-step, goal-oriented tasks independently and successfully.

behaviours.name: Familiar Routine Strategies
behaviours.description:
• Engages in elements of routine tasks using familiar methods and strategies.
  • May participate in new tasks or activitiesthrough observation or with supportive prompts.
  • May require repetition and tailored guidance tofeel confident with new tasks or activities.

behaviours.name: Adapts to Small Changes
behaviours.description:
• Adapts when small changes are made to daily routines (e.g., a change to the order of activities).
  • Adapts to a small change made midway through an activity or game, (e.g., when a peer suggests a different way to build the tower).
  • Investigatesunfamiliar learning environments or materials through observation.
  • Participates in simple activities where thinking is required, and extensive practice and supportive cuesare provided.
  • Participates in tasks where trial and error are involved, with opportunities to experiment and try out a simple hypothesis.

behaviours.name: Adapts With Support
behaviours.description:
• Adapts to major changes to daily routines with support (e.g., switching from indoor playtime to an unexpected trip outdoors), though may still become slightly over-excited, or anxious.
  • Adapts to a complex change made mid-way through an activity or game (e.g., shifting from building anaeroplane with blocks to building a car).
  • Investigatesunfamiliar learning environments or materials (may tend to focus on a few elements only or areas more in line with their interests).
  • Participates in tasks using one or two strategies, to try out a hypothesis with some guidance.

behaviours.name: Flexible Independent Adaptation
behaviours.description:
• Adapts to major changes in daily routines without requiring support (e.g., adjusting to relief staff when familiar teachers and educators are absent).
  • Flexibly adapts to multiple changes throughout an activity or game (e.g., switching roles or tasks in a group project as needed).
  • Thoroughly investigatesnew contexts or materials, seeking opportunities to explore new environments and resources to extend their learning.
  • Participates in tasks where they can engage in using higher order thinking skills, confidently experimenting and formulating their own hypotheses needing minimal guidance.

behaviours.name: Adapts for Peers
behaviours.description: • Demonstrates flexibility in response to their peers’ needs (e.g., invents rules to make the game easier or harder for different players).

behaviours.name: Supported Simple Strategies
behaviours.description:
• Engages with simple problem-solving strategies with some adult support (e.g., finding a card, looking at a picture).
  • Focuses on the sensory aspects of a problem or task, rather than applying problem-solving strategies to identify the actual problem (e.g., notices that their clothes feel cold when wet but does not identify the need to change out of wet clothes).
  • May not independently identify simple, routine problems without adult guidance.

behaviours.name: Familiar Problem Strategies
behaviours.description:
• Demonstrates predictable or familiar strategies for how a routine problem might be solved (e.g., what do we do if our drink bottle is empty?).
  • Attempts to experiment with new and unfamiliar materials, (e.g., incorporating unexpected elements into a process).
  • Shares how they attempted to solve a problem (may not reflect what actually happened).
  • Makes a simple plan (e.g., a role play of a familiar fairytale) that involves some collaboration or sequencing but is largely spontaneous.
  • Engages with aspects of an activity for sensory exploration or enjoyment, without necessarily seeking a solution (e.g., builds a block tower, enjoying the sound of it falling down and not viewing the ‘falling tower’ as a problem).

behaviours.name: Guided Strategy Reflection
behaviours.description:
• Demonstrates1-2 strategiesto solve a problem usingunfamiliar materials (e.g., construction of a three-dimensional object using recycled materials).
  • Experiments with at least two different, familiar strategiesto solve a problem. (e.g., how to negotiate a play space with peers, but requires some hints and ideas to try more imaginative and potentially effective approaches from a teacher or educator).
  • Describes, with prompting, some aspects of how they attempted to solve a problem. When prompted they can reflect on what worked or did not work, reflecting on the strategies they used.
  • Plans an activity with a peer that happens across different timeswithin one day (e.g., putting on a simple play including assigning roles, simple costumes, and a basic plot) as well as adjusting to minor disruptions (e.g., a conflict between two children wanting the same costume).
  • Reflects on their own responses and may change their solution in response to feedback.

behaviours.name: Flexible Problem Planning
behaviours.description:
• Demonstratesseveral strategiesfor how an unfamiliar problem might be solved (e.g., how to dismantle a piece of unfamiliar equipment with spanners and screwdrivers).
  • Experiments with diverse and imaginative ways to solve an unfamiliar problem (e.g., incorporating new and unfamiliar resources into an activity).
  • Describes how they attempted to solve a problem providing some reasons for what they did, or did not do, why it worked or did not work and what they might do differently next time.
  • Collaboratively plan and sequence an activity with peers that involves a series of steps to take place over two or more days (e.g., putting on an elaborate play with a detailed plot and consideration of how to engage the audience), including adjusting to disruptions and unanticipated events (e.g., rain during an outdoor scene in the play).

behaviours.name: Leads Problem Solving
behaviours.description:
• Leads others through strategiesto problems.
  • Provides a range of strategies for different contexts.
  • Provides solutions that challenge existing rules or conventionalways of doing things (e.g., rewrites rules in games to increase their challenge) or to make them easier for others.
